text: Welcome back to Immigration News Today.
If you are planning to fly in or out of
the United States in 2026, you need to
stop and watch this video before you
pack a single item. I'm not exaggerating
when I say that what we're about to
cover could be the difference between
catching your flight and being pulled
aside for a secondary screening that
could take hours or worse, having items
confiscated, being issued a civil
penalty, or in some cases being flagged
in a federal database. That is not a
scare tactic. That is the reality of how
seriously US airport security has
evolved. And the rules going into effect
including updates aligned with guidance
taking effect around March 1st, are
something every traveler needs to
understand. So today, we're breaking
down five specific categories of items
that you should not keep in your bag the
way you may have been keeping them. Some
of these rules are updated, some are
more strictly enforced than ever before,
and some are genuinely new. We're going
to go through each one carefully,
explain what the rule is, why it exists,
what happens if you get caught with it,
and exactly what you should do instead.
This is the kind of information that
saves you time, money, stress, and in
certain situations can protect your
immigration status and your right to
travel. So stay with me all the way
through because each of these five items
matters. Before we dive in, a quick
note. As of February 26, 2026, all
information in this video reflects
current guidance from the Transportation
Security Administration, which is TSA,
the Department of Homeland Security, or
DHS, and US Customs and Border
Protection, known as CBP. Immigration
and airport security policy can change,
and it often does with little advanced
notice to the general public. So, please
always verify the most current rules
directly on the official TSA website at
tsa.gov,
the CBP website at cbp.gov, and your
specific airlines baggage policies
before you travel. Nothing in this video
is legal advice, and if you have
questions specific to your immigration
status or travel history, please consult
a qualified immigration attorney or
accredited representative. With that
said, let's get into it. The first item,
and this one trips up more travelers
than almost anything else in 2026, is
lithium batteries. Now, you might be
thinking, "I've been flying with my
phone and my laptop for years. No one
has ever stopped me." And you're right.
Those are fine. But here's what's
changed and here is what matters. The
TSA has significantly tightened its
enforcement around loose spare or
external lithium batteries. This
includes power banks, external battery
packs, spare batteries for cameras,
ecigarette batteries, and replacement
batteries for electronic devices. These
items have always had restrictions, but
what we're seeing in 2026 is a much
higher rate of enforcement at checkpoint
screening lanes with TSA officers
actively flagging bags that contain
loose lithium batteries in checked
luggage. Here is the key rule you need
to know. Lithium ion and lithium metal
batteries that are spare or loose,
meaning not installed in a device, are
prohibited in checked baggage. They must
be in your carry-on. And even in
carry-on, there are WWh limits. For
consumer electronics, like your phone
battery or your laptop, you're generally
fine if the battery is 100 W hours or
less. For batteries between 100 and 160
W hours, you need airline approval.
Above 160W hours, they are typically not
allowed at all. Power banks fall under
these same rules. If you've got a large
capacity power bank sitting loose at the
bottom of your checked suitcase, you are
going to have a problem. TSA has the
authority to confiscate it. And if the
battery is deemed a fire hazard, you
could face additional scrutiny. What
should you do right now? Go through your
checked bag and pull out every single
battery that is not physically installed
in a device. every power bank, every
spare camera battery, every ecigarette
device that has a removable lithium
cell. Put those in your carry-on clearly
organized and ideally in the original
packaging or in a protective case that
prevents shortcircuiting.
This is not optional. The Federal
Aviation Administration, the FAA, has
flagged lithium battery fires as one of
the leading causes of in-flight
emergencies, and TSA enforcement
reflects that priority in 2026. The
second item, and this one is going to
surprise a lot of people, particularly
those who pack food for long journeys,
is food and powder-based items. In 2026,
TSA is applying much more consistent
enforcement of its powder rule, which
was introduced a few years ago, but is
now being applied uniformly at airports
across the country. Here is the rule.
Any powder, granular substance, or
finely ground material in your carry-on
luggage that exceeds 12 oz or
approximately 350 ml must be removed
from your bag and placed in a separate
bin for X-ray screening.
If TSA cannot determine what the powder
is through screening, they may ask you
to dispose of it or they may subject you
to additional testing. Now, this covers
a wide range of things people carry.
Protein powder, spices, ground coffee,
baby formula, cremated remains, sand or
soil souvenirs, powdered vitamins. All
of these, if they exceed 12 o in your
carry-on, need to come out of the bag
and go in a bin. For items like baby
formula and medically necessary powders,
there are exemptions, but you are still
expected to declare them and separate
them for screening. The TSA website has
specific guidance on traveling with
medically necessary items. And I
strongly encourage you to read that page
before you fly if you regularly carry
any kind of powder-based product. What
this means practically is that if you
are packing protein shakes, meal
replacement powders, or even large
quantities of ground spices in your
carry-on, you need to either move them
to checked luggage, where these
restrictions are much less stringent, or
be prepared to remove them at the
checkpoint and have them screened
separately. The worst thing you can do
is leave a large container of
unidentified white or tan powder buried
at the bottom of your carry-on bag. that
is almost guaranteed to trigger a bag
search, slow you down, and potentially
cause you to miss your flight. The third
item is undeclared currency and
financial instruments. And this is one
where the consequences can be truly
severe, especially for international
travelers and those on non-immigrant
visas. Here is what you need to
understand. If you are traveling
internationally, arriving in the United
States, or departing, and you are
carrying $10,000 or more in cash,
monetary instruments, travelers checks,
or certain negotiable instruments, you
are legally required to declare it. This
is not a suggestion. This is a federal
law enforced by US Customs and Border
Protection. The form used for this
declaration when entering the country is
the fins 105 and for international
arrivals. It is also captured through
the CBP declaration form. What has
changed in 2026 is not the threshold.
That $10,000 figure has been in place
for years. But the enforcement CBP is
using more advanced financial profiling
and cash detection technology at ports
of entry. And there is a growing
emphasis on catching travelers who
structure their currency to stay just
under that reporting threshold, which is
itself a federal offense called
structuring. If you arrive with $9800 in
cash and a CBP officer has reason to
believe you were attempting to avoid the
reporting requirement, that money can be
seized and you can face civil or
criminal penalties for immigration
purposes. This is especially important
to understand if you are on a visa, a
green card, or in any status other than
a US citizen. Having your currency
seized or being investigated for a
financial crime at the border can have
downstream consequences on your
immigration case, your ability to adjust
status, or your admissibility to the
United States. So, the rule is simple.
If you are carrying $10,000 or more in
any combination of cash or financial
instruments, declare it. It is not
illegal to carry large amounts of cash.
It is illegal to not declare it.
Declaring it does not automatically
result in confiscation. It means you are
following the law. And as a practical
step right now, if you regularly travel
internationally with significant sums of
cash, perhaps because you are sending
money to family, conducting business, or
for other legitimate reasons, consider
consulting with an immigration attorney
or a financial adviser who understands
crossber currency rules. They can help
you understand how to document the
source of funds, which is something CBP
may ask about. The fourth item is
something that has become dramatically
more relevant in 2026, and that is
certain agricultural and food products,
particularly those brought in from
abroad. The United States has some of
the strictest agricultural inspection
rules in the world, enforced by the US
Department of Agriculture, the USDA, in
coordination with CBP.
And while these rules are not new, what
is new is the level of technology and
the number of trained agriculture
inspection dogs and officers deployed at
major airports.
The inspection rate for incoming
international flights at hub airports
like JFK, LAX, Miami, and O'Hare has
increased significantly. So what
specifically are we talking about? Fresh
fruits and vegetables. Many of them are
either prohibited or require specific
documentation and inspection to be
brought into the United States from
abroad. Meats, poultry, and eggs from
many countries are prohibited without
specific permits. Soil, plants with
roots, and certain seeds are also
heavily restricted. The issue is that
many travelers, particularly those
visiting family, or bringing culturally
significant foods from home, do not
realize that these restrictions apply to
them. Bringing a mango from the
Philippines, a piece of homemade cheese
from Mexico, or a bag of spices from
India might seem harmless, but if those
items trigger an agricultural inspection
and are found to carry pests or
pathogens, you face a significant fine
and the items will be destroyed. Here is
the key action item. Before you pack any
food, plant material, or soil related
item in your luggage when traveling
internationally to the United States, go
to the USDA APHIS website, that's the
Animal and Plant Health Inspection
Service, and check their specific
guidance for what is and is not allowed
from the country you are traveling from.
When in doubt, declare it on your CBP
declaration form. Declaring an
agricultural item is not the same as
admitting wrongdoing. CBP officers can
inspect it and tell you whether it can
be admitted, but if you fail to declare
something that is later found during
screening, the fine for a first offense
can be several hundred. And for repeated
or intentional violations, the penalties
increase substantially. The fifth item,
and this is the one that has the most
direct immigration implication, is
travel documents that are improperly
stored or presented. Now, I know that
might sound like it does not quite fit
the category of things in your bag, but
stay with me because this is critically
important for anyone who is not a US
citizen. In 2026, CBP is continuing to
roll out and expand its biometric and
document verification systems at ports
of entry. This includes facial
recognition technology now operating at
a large number of international
terminals and an expansion of the
electronic verification of travel
documents against federal databases.
What this means practically is that
discrepancies between your physical
documents and what is on file in federal
systems are being caught much more
quickly and consistently than they used
to be. If you are carrying a passport
that has an incorrect name, a visa with
a typo, an I94 that does not match your
actual entry date, or if you have an
expired green card that you have not yet
renewed, these issues are going to
surface at the checkpoint. And the time
to discover those issues is not when you
are standing in front of a CBP officer
at 2:00 a.m. after a 14-hour flight.
Here is what you should do before every
international trip. Go through all of
your travel documents. Check your
passport's expiration date. Most
countries require that your passport be
valid for at least 6 months beyond your
intended stay. Check your visa to
confirm it is still valid and that the
conditions match your purpose of travel.
If you are a green card holder, check
the expiration date on your card. While
a green card does not expire in the same
way a visa does, your permanent resident
status remains even if the card itself
expires. CBP requires a valid unexpired
card for re-entry. If your green card is
expired or about to expire, file your
form I90 with USCIS before you travel.
Also, check your I94 record online. You
can do this right now at
i94.cbp.ths.gov.
Your I94 is your official record of
admission to the United States, and it
determines how long you are authorized
to stay. Many travelers, particularly
those who have traveled multiple times
or who received I94s on paper years ago,
have I94 discrepancies that they do not
even know about. Catching those issues
before you travel and consulting with an
immigration attorney if something looks
wrong is far better than being surprised
at a port of entry. Now, let us talk
about the broader picture of what all of
this means heading into 2026.
Airport security and customs enforcement
in the United States have become
significantly more technologically
sophisticated. Artificial intelligence
is being used to analyze passenger data
before you even board your plane. The
TSA pre-check and global entry programs,
which allow for expedited screening, are
seeing record enrollment, but they also
come with enhanced vetting. CBP's
automated targeting system assigns risk
scores to travelers based on travel
history, financial patterns, and
database information. The goal of all of
this is to identify genuine threats, and
keep travel safe. But as a traveler,
your job is to make sure that your bag,
your documents, and your behavior at the
checkpoint do not inadvertently raise
flags that slow you down or in serious
cases affect your ability to enter or
remain in the United States. So, let's
quickly recap the five things. First,
loose lithium batteries in checked
luggage. Move them to carryon or leave
them home. Second, large quantities of
powder or granular substances in
carry-on without separation. Either
check them or be ready to remove them at
the checkpoint. Third, undeclared
currency of $10,000 or more. Always
declare it. Always document the source.
Fourth, prohibited or undeclared
agricultural and food items from
international origins. Check the USDA
APHIS website before you pack and always
declare on your CBP form. Fifth,
improperly maintained, expired, or
mismatched travel documents. Audit every
document before every trip and address
any discrepancies before you get to the
airport. These are not abstract rules.
Every single week, travelers are
delayed, penalized, or worse because of
one of these five issues. And in many
cases, a few minutes of preparation
before leaving home would have prevented
the entire problem. If you found this
video helpful, please take a moment
right now to give it a like and share it
with someone you know who travels to or
from the United States. That simple
action helps more people find this
information before they need it, not
after. And if you are not yet subscribed
to Immigration News Today, please hit
that subscribe button and turn on
notifications.

holder, a visa holder, a new arrival, or
someone planning a trip to the United
States for the first time, the
description below this video includes
links to official sources we referenced
today. TSA gov for TSA screening rules,
cbp.gov for customs and border
protection guidance, the USDA's
website for agricultural import rules,
and the I94.cbp.dhs.
dhs.gov portal to check your admission
record. Please use those resources. They
are free, they are official, and they
are updated regularly. As always, a

forformational purposes only as of
February 26th, 2026.
It does not constitute legal advice, and

If you have a specific situation

so much for being part of the
